This one is less talked about and it might be the most important thing. Emotional intelligence is not just saying the right words. It's sensing what people are feeling, reading what's not being said and responding in a way that helps people feel understood. AI can generate empathy, flavored language, but it cannot genuinely notice a crack in somebody's voice, the hesitation that exists before they answer your question, the defensive shift in their body language, or the silent tension that changes the whole conversation. That is emotional intelligence. It's knowing when to push and when to pause. It's knowing when you need to reassure people and when someone needs clarity instead of encouragement. In the age of AI, the people who rise will not just be the ones with better answers. They will be the ones who can make people feel seen, heard and understood. And skill three is communicating from lived experience. AI can write a moving story, but it cannot tell the truth of a life that it never lived. AI can generate a story about failure, but it has never felt. It can write about pressure, but it actually has never felt pressure.
